steps.title: Identify what the data must show
steps.content: The passage contrasts three breeds: mouflon (wild ancestor), Merino (outer nearly as soft as inner), and Navajo-Churro (selected to keep the coarse outer/fine inner distinction). The word yet signals a surprising finding. We need a statement that, using the table's inner fiber diameters, reveals something unexpected when all three are compared. The table gives minimum inner fiber diameters: Navajo-Churro 10 μm, Mouflon 15 μm, Merino 17 μm.

steps.title: Eliminate wrong choices, confirm the match
steps.content:
• Choice A — 'the selection process that enabled the Navajo-Churro to retain its somewhat coarse outer fiber also resulted in inner fiber that, at its softest, is softer than either the mouflon’s or the Merino’s inner fiber.': 10 < 15 < 17 — Navajo-Churro’s minimum inner diameter is the smallest of all three, so its softest inner fiber is finer (softer) than either rival. This matches the “yet” surprise. ✓
  • Choice B — 'the Navajo-Churro more closely resembles its ancestor, the mouflon, in the uniform softness of its inner fiber...': The table shows Navajo-Churro inner fiber ranges 10–35 μm — highly variable, not uniform. The data contradict this. ✗
  • Choice C — 'domestication resulted in a counterintuitive increase in the inner fiber’s minimum diameter...': Navajo-Churro’s minimum inner diameter decreased to 10 μm from mouflon’s 15 μm, so this is factually wrong for that breed. ✗
  • Choice D — 'domestication...resulted in greater softness of outer and inner fiber alike.': Merino inner fiber minimum is 17 μm vs. mouflon’s 15 μm — Merino inner is less soft than mouflon’s, so the claim fails for inner fiber. ✗
That matches choice A — A is correct.
